Finally we returned to Lugano and took the train back home.

When I was about twelve or thirteen I took a trip with my aunts. We)
left Beatenberg for Interlaken by carriage in the morning, then crossed the _
Brienzersee by boat, landed in Meiringen, thence to Alpnach and Lucerne}.
by way of the Briinig Pass. On the next day, to Vitznau, and on the third, +

back to Lucerne and to Bern by way of Langnau.

When about fifteen, a student trip with Professor Tobler. I. Bern-Grimsel-f
hospiz; II. Grimselpass Glacier, by carriage to Fiesch, on foot to Hotel Eg-F

324 / Diary III

cases. I was ready to look for causes with him, but could not indulge in searching for hypothetical, basic presuppositions.

In this state of waiting and hoping, the telegram announcing his death
struck me like lightning. It was sent by his wife from Bonn, where she was
staying with the widow of August Macke. Ominous urge, on her part, to
journey there and breathe the same air.

The telegram called me to Frankfurt; Frau Marc had had other company
that far. She could not bear to be alone. On the same day I received the red
slip informing me that I would be mustered in on March 11th. That night I
decided to clean up things a bit and emptied a number of drawers, thereby
disturbing my wife in her sleep. At last I thought of her and went to bed; then
the bell rang, shrill and long, the way it rings at night when an urgent telegram is delivered. With a sense of impending disaster, I went—I don’t remember. how-to the.garden gate, and the catastrophe occurred.

On the next morning I left for Frankfurt. Frau Marc was there at the station. She had stayed with a family named Hirschfeld. We came back on the
night train and arrived home at nine in the morning. :

On the following days more detailed information arrived from the front, as
well as Marc’s last two letters to his wife. Moreover, people kept coming in
and out of our apartment to offer condolences to the widow. :

Prepared in this way, I packed my bag and went to the District Command
on March 11th.

Now I have a new position in life: I am Infantry Reservist Klee, and my
address is: Recruits’ Depot Landshut, Section Gabelsbergerhof.
LANDSHUT 1916

966. Infantry Reservist Klee, Recr
bergerhof.

Early on the morning of March 1ith, I went with my suitcase to the
District Command barracks (District Command, Munich I). At least the
weather was splendid. After we had been assembled there
structions written on blackboards,
awhile. A kindly major wished us

uits’ Depot Landshut, Section Gabels-

luck on our journey. We were told that we
| Were going to Landshut. And yet I had tried to get permission to remain in

Munich. I found it humiliating (it was the last day for a long time on which I
was supposed to have an opinion about anything)
At about one in the afternoon, some noncommissioned officers arrived,
carrying guns, and escorted us to the railroad station, People celebrating in the
streets along the route, Close relatives, Daughters, Tears, Jokes. A special
i I snap up a few consoling words. “Con-

i our town. There isn’t a better place any-

_ At about 4:30, arrived in Landshut. Again four by four. We are told to
march on the sidewalk. From a schoolhouse, the sound of the ironic laughter
of troops at the windows. A halt in the Heissgarten. There, a good deal of
calling of roll takes place. Our passports are taken from us. The sections are
made up. A sergeant with a good head takes charge of us. Upon reaching our
quarters we are divided, according to our height, into five detachments, all
the while standing in the street. Night is beginning to fall when at last we
get into our quarters, a large room in an inn, with a bowling alley. The
corporal apologizes in complicated language about the poor quarters. A dark
table, with rows of straw sacks, on each one a loaf of bread, two plates, and
_ knife and fork. Narrow alleys between the straw pallets. Very hungry. No

eal dinner. Noncommissioned officers try to cheer us up. A few days later, we
ill receive permission to sleep in town.

That night at the inn amidst smoke, headache (
ausages, and beer. Then a most sensational night
awakened by a harsh “On your feet!”
Sundays ever.

the last for a long time),
. In the morning we are
beginning one of the most memorable

327
328 / Diary IV 329 / March 1916 to December 1918

Sunday, 3.12., awakened at six. Had a little sleep, more the result of sadness
than anything else. Marched to breakfast. At first nauseated at the sight of
Then we marched through the old town, very brave and stiff, four by four, to
the Prantlgarten. Under different circumstances the city would be enchanting.
At the Glaspalast we’re outfitted. Again terribly nauseated by these rags and
straps, half civilian, half military, loaded down with gear, having trouble
holding onto everything, convulsively, painfully pack-saddled with greasy
pieces of uniform, boots, undefinable leather objects, dragging stuff along in
the mud, running the gauntlet, convicts exhibited for the curiosity of Sunday
strollers, we march back. At our quarters, dress rehearsal. The play promises to
bea fine one. od

Then, for the first time, going for chow. The food plentiful. Breakfast had
already been survived. Delightful. The fat blond pig that dishes out the coffee.
His unerring sureness. Ordered around in impossible boots, to our quarters,
to bathe—to exchange the boots, a fruitless operation. Finally, the deep
melancholy of the prisoner. Dull Sunday afternoon. :

70. 3.16. Today we took our rifles along on our hike. It isn’t easy the first
ime. But after a few drills out in the open, i

lost my feet, but had acquired in their place a head of lead. Someone named
‚Köster is said to have had the luck to be assigned to a Reserve Regiment in
Munich. This may make me somewhat thoughtful, for Lily’s sake, But the
going is stiffer there, and the chances of being sent to the front are greater,
This is a good place for such a rest cure,

Sometimes Marc’s name comes to min

¢ d, I am struck and I see something
collapsing.

967. Monday, 3.13. Today, drill; with my own boots, it would have been a
pleasure. As it was, I suffered from the melancholy caused by sore feet. As of
tonight, unfortunately, no mail from home. How do they feel without me, I
wonder. Are they worried? :

Thave never been so free of worries. Doing what you are ordered to do is not
at all difficult. The cuisine isn’t French, but it tastes all right and there is
enough of it. What a novelty this hunger and thirst is. Downing a quart of
beer without scruple!

First evening off, alone. Great apprehension of encountering a superior in’

nk. Unfortunately we are not allowed to go to Munich next Sunday. I ad-

‘vised my wife to come to Landshut for t

he time being. My smudgy uniform
‘as one of the reasons, " *

Ni

1. Friday, 3.17. On Saturday I found some enjoyment in the hike. An untivaled cure. The first mental relaxation in a long time.

Ishall show myself coöperative at the beginning; in this way no one will be
on my back, and the mood won’t go to pieces too quickly,

Groner, the noncommissioned officer, jokes about my heavy correspondence with my wife. I ask you to try to maintain your military bearing in
_ your postcards. “Herr von Klee, your wife is on the telephone. She says you’ ve
. got to come home.”

My answer: “Sorry, Sir. I don’t know how
I knew then that he read my cards,

968. 3.14. Mail at last! What this means! Only the tone is too subdued
Felix praises Sophie’s talent for cooking. Lily wants to try to get me trans
ferred back to Munich. I’ll stay in Landshut. I was lucky. We are one of the
oldest classes called in. In Munich we might be put together with young men.
Here, we are leftovers, second-hand goods, The tone is leisurely. Soon I'll be. |
able to live in town. On Saturdays one can take the train to Munich. But this
coming Saturday is doubtful because of the lack of field-gray uniforms.

to use the phone.”
330 / Diary IV 331 / March 1916 to December 1918

Task that my box of watercolors bi

I have rented a room, five marks per week, right next to the camp. Ele,
P g P & ans... e sent tome... Small hopes—illu-

sunny, with a fine view. Gas, etc. Actually, I don’t need a nice room for m)
few hours of liberty, but it will be very useful when my family visits me. —

3.22. The weather is cooler, we hay

972. 3.18. There was no trickery about the cancellation of the first furlough: nated against smallpox

it was shame at the absence of the gray uniforms. I tell my family to com
after all, this Sunday, although my uniform looks like that of a convict, I tel
Lily to bring my civilian clothes, so that we may spend four or five happy hour Drill in marksmanshi
together as usual. We shall make tea and eat cake. As usual. In the afternoo e only problem is m
great cleaning up. We are photographed, detachment after detachment. i

3.19. Sunday. Went to church in convict’s garb. In the afternoon, Lil
and Felix came. Pleasant hours.

973. The swarthy fellow from Dachau, somewhat isolated in this assembl and offers to take care of m
of Munich rascals, attaches himself to me; when I turn toward him and as
him a few questions, his eyes shine. He was taken away from farm work, A
cleaning woman, a war widow, has thus been put in a most distressing situ
tion. She will ask for him to be sent back. Now he stands about here and h
to stiffen his knees and glue his hands to his sides.

4 A few times I let the man from Dachau have some bread, whereupon he
lecides, without asking me, to clean my boots. A fine man, even in his jester’s
costume. Torn away from the earth.

978. 3.23. Filled out the passes for Saturday/Sund
Monday before passed off quick ‘of it he, ol wm A

974. 3.20. Drill from 7:30 until 11 o’clock. The sun is burning. Address i ( ly, but . .
sight fever at night. % Out Part of it has stuck in my larynx, A

teal Bavarian by the first lieutenant. Always the same patriotic formula
Speeches one could and would make oneself if one had to listen to them agai

a few times. 979. 3.24. The one-year men receive their

others are shamelessly allowed to go to Mu
the first time in many years,
Unfortunately I have a fever

gray uniforms today at noon. The
nich in indescribable fatigues. For

975. 3.21. Inspection by the Commanding General v. d. Tann. We hike my academic education is of some use to
me.

(in boots!) quite a distance, Got up at 5 o’clock. At 5:30, “grabbed” brea
fast. Then, quickly grabbed our rifles. To the Heissgarten, after a long wai
then back to our quarters. There, waited. Then, back to the Heissgarten. I
spection of equipment. Instruction about how to behave during inspectio :
and, possibly, address, Marched off at last at 7:30. Went out at 9 o'clock.

Dress rehearsal. Lieutenant Buhl plays the part of His Excellency. After th
performance, light drill. Acquired two blisters and bruises on my elbow (th
uifle). A hero, Light rain sprinkles us and the dusty road, :

, not given leave, Consequence: I per-

, terrain drill, that is, a walk
then, as the weather brightened up, into the hill h

Enchanting country,

in the rain;

y region south of Landshut.
with glimpses of the lovely town. :

N 327. “My dear little boy, now I’ve got it easy. The doctor noticed that
ad a fever, and now I am allowed to stay in my nice room at 12/II Gabels332 / Diary IV
= 333 / March 1916 to December 1918

bergerstrasse. I only show up for meals, and everyone understands. But:o

Thursday matters become serious again. Then I shall want to be quite health

again. Farewell, do your work first, then you may play and be happy.”

82. 3.28. From time to time I
ainting. Read

981. 3.27. A slight temperature (100 degrees) impelled the doctor to gi
me three days of sick leave. He was not at all interested in the kind’o
fection; he imagined at first that it was caused by my teeth. I protested fair
energetically, because otherwise he might have sent me to the dentist, Th
whole business of going to the doctor, a new sensation. First, assembly ino
quarters, put on our coats because it is still March. And stand around. The
we are led by a private first class to the Heissgarten, where the sick are 1
quired to carry back the coffee bucket. One of them, it turned out later, h:
a 104° fever. Then an assembly of the individual barracks units in the Hei
garten; this meant a great deal of standing around with occasional saluti
thrown in. All sorts of talks, all sorts of orders, until we are marched off |
fours by a private from the sanitary corps. To get out of the way of a regul
marching detachment, we execute a column-right maneuver, the fellow wit
the 104° fever too, and walk on the sidewalk for a while. At last we rea
our goal, the Martin School. Then to the infirmary, to have our temperal N
taken. What a marvelous infirmary! The air! Fresh air comes in thr
exactly one window pane, because it is broken, or maybe it’s really to allow
sick man to converse with someone standing in the courtyard? Pants dow
three by three, we lie down to have our temperatures taken. The fellow wi
the 104 degrees is discovered and held. Men with less than 104 degrees mu
go out into the corridor again and line up three by three. We are waiting foı
doctor; we have been on our feet since seven, and it is nine when the doct
begins his tour of inspection through the infirmary. Actually this doesn’t
long. Those with high temperatures go with the doctor into the consult
room and are examined first. Shortly after, they are taken to the infirm N
breathe the stuffy air for a while. A stiff punishment could be no worse th
this. I have reason to feel contented: I am let off with three days in bed.
retire to my quarters (which is more quickly said than done, since all sorts.
formalities must be gone through yet) and only leave them twice a day:
my two bowls. I continue to receive my pay . . . This is what is called: goi
to the doctor. Instructive, impressive, and sometimes rewarding. , We are to detect enemy snipers in the distance,

and fire away. The captain poked me

3. 3.30. First evening without fever. Played music valiantly. No loss of

chnique. T'omorrow sick leave will eld to outine a; w
hi I tot iu But to b
q yl he rou gain. But to be well

984. 3.31. Snow fell early in the mornin
wasn’t frozen. I am over it. The swelli
y night drill. Sounds serious, And w

g, and for the first time in a week,
ngs ın my mouth are healing, Toill certainly turn out to be some-

sections.title: Pages 21–29
sections.text:
334 / Diary IV

335 / March 1916 to December 1918

from behind with the point of his sword: “What are you aiming at, friend?

aspar on leave. A distracted, sluggish man. And we stand and shiver like
He was amazed at my correct answer and moved away embarrassed.

aspen leaves. During the break, we run around like animals about to be fed.

Afterward the circus band comes to fetch us. Slim consolation for what we
987. Singing instructions are no longer given by the clear-voiced sergeant, 4 ‚had endured,
but by Corporal Bruckner. A neat man with a slight squint that doesn’ :
look bad. First we all read the text together, then he sings the first stanz
fearfully off-key, so that our ears cringe. Then we sing it. Today we learned a
horrible piece of trash called “Flag Song,” music-hall stuff.

I am living with apes. I realize this seeing them take this unadulterated ru
bish with such seriousness. And yet, we are all brothers, Everybody wants
go home. Even the officers. :

How Corporal Weidl laughed at the fat, rosy Jew Karlsen because at la
arich man was caught. 8

| 991. 4.13. Under sharp wind and icy showers, hiked an hour toward Salzdorf.
we formed firing lines in the forest and crawled
through the wet moss like salamanders, Waited for a particularly heavy downPour. Then out to look at a trench. The sergeant scrutinizes, criticizes, and
explains it. The wind makes us freeze more than it dries us. A sharp glance
ftom the sun and new showers. Hundred-pound slippers made of yellow loam.
~ Sergeant Pritzlmeier, a teacher of mathematics at the Anna School, often
tells about his experiences on the front in intelligent, not unpleasan

fashion,
4.7. Announced imy absence at the orchestra rehearsal of Creation on a But then again he can be quite tough.
count of a long hike, : a
992. 4.14. For the first time in ages, followed instruction without having to
988. 4.10. Monday mood. Still half in Munich. In the ‚morning, at the ba

fight against sleep; the course, given by a little Corporal named Huber, dealt
with marksmanship and military conduct. What is the talk of even the
brightest lieutenant compared to such homespun “discoursing.” And besides,
the shrewd mouse face of the little fellow. The climax came when he urged us
‚not to act timid in front of superiors: “They're made of flesh too, they’re all
made of flesh!”
Instead of Kaspar, we are trained by a delicate, blond little man, He must
have had the most frightful experiences at the front. Completely lost in his
own thoughts, he forgets in no time the orders received from the lieutenan
| ‘or the vice-sergeant-major. He dreams away and, at every opportunity, brings

up stories about the front. We listen, enjoy ourselves, but learn nothing. No
one until now suspects how incapable this blond chap will prove himself on
the drillground. He can’t find his way out of the real way into this game played
| ona green meadow. His name is Schuster,
| _ The brothers got wet today. Tomorrow is marksmanship day.

| 415. I first shot twelve, then less; took aim lying down, one hundred and
4 fifty meters.
990. 4.12, Weather suddenly took a change for the worse; there was an icy |

draft on the drill ground, and I froze terribly. Corporal Schuster replaces | s 993. 4.16. Solemn oath-taking shindig. Heard a beautiful mass with music,

racks, cleaned my equipment a bit. We were to be there one hour befo
marching time. Until then we had circumvented the foolish regulation. B
we hadn’t managed to do away with it once and for all. I think mostly of th
coming leave for the Easter holidays. On Palm Sunday we have to stay her
swearing-in ceremony. :

Today, rifle practice, during which one doesn’t move at all, and the momi
was so very cold! Almost an hour on our bellies, then another half hour
standing and kneeling in the open. Quick-aiming drill (triangular target
Then three shots with blank cartridges.

989. 4.11. Radiant day, stood out in the sun; weapon training and other
silly stuff, In the evening, marched until night had fallen. Whither? How far?

Wrote Kubin about the works that Marc had left behind. Went to the post
office, picked up five marks. Gave several hundred salutes on the way and, in |
the old town, walked a distance through arcades just like those in Bern.
336 / Diary IV

337 / March 1916 to December 1918

Visit from Lily and Felix. At the last minute, changed my room. N;
Klötzlmüllerstrasse 16, Lily dissatisfied with the room, a bit annoying—too
much of a lady.

„ine quarters with envy. Later, two more came with the lieutenant’s order
that we return to our field positions at 8:10,

“unless express counterorders
 zeached us.”

From there we went back to base singing.
In the morning, it was biting cold out in the mud-bowl. In the afternoon

© learned the glorious song “When the Soldiers March Through Town”
nan hour.

994. 4.17. Second marskmanship drill, lying at two hundred meters, hands
free. I shot eleven, then eight. At noon, was called to the administratic
bureau. Had a look at the one-year enlistment blank. _
The old.slave-dance. Gun-cleaning, slogging in the field, and singing will
go on for a few more days, then a week of bliss (Easter leave). And: then?
Even world wars end. _
4.18. They say that one oughtn’t to shoot so well. Should I drag along the
special sights, as the poor marksmen are forced to do? :
We leave very early on Thursday morning by special train. Assembly at
six o'clock in the Heissgarten.

4.24. On Saturday afternoon, vaccination. I shall probably miss the 5:14
vening train, but shall certainly catch the express train at 8:22 or the local
at 8:36. We shall therefore not see each other until night.

‚95b. 4.27. Good trip, slept well, Some drilling, really more like calisthenics.

Climbed up the pillar of a bridge and down again on the other side. Medical
inspection, Instruction.

gsc. 5.1. Today we had to carry our packs.

5.2, Our work is now rendered slightly harder by the warm weather and
y our load of equipment. This afternoon we shall have a long march with
our new gear on our backs. We shall see.

5.3. A seventeen-mile hike with a full load
test that was! A dozen men in our column c
by the side of the road, with a heart attack,
for less than an hour;

995. The Easter leave began for me in high spirits. At six o’clock in the
Heissgarten. We march off to the train, Climb aboard cattle cars lined with
benches. Instead of doors, a cross-bar. Extremely drafty. To make up, there‘
no fare to pay. Besides, we get one mark and a half for expenses every day.
Also, we continue to receive our regular pay, thirty-three pfennigs a day. - =

A few days in Munich. Then the gala for the opening of the Weisgerbei
exhibition. Then a trip to Wiessee for a few days. Ate and slept well. Still
not quite in my normal state. Couldn’t help it, and it wasn’t so bad after all
Going back after the furlough was quite painful. One had tasted freedom
again.

, yesterday—what a grueling
ollapsed. I saw one of them lying
This morning, a hike in the hills
then lay about in the forest, with marvelous weather,
nd took under an hour coming back, I guess things will be all right.

95d. 5.4. I sat yesterday with a few select companions at a nearby inn
called The New World. A splendid garden and a chance to get a couple of
eggs and some rolls, This afternoon we hiked out to the most distant of the
illgrounds, an hour and a quarter’s walk with our full load on our backs,
Out there we had strenuous drill conducted by the captain. Then the hike
back home! In the heat. Now that it’s over, I must say it went off all right,
but painfully, painfully!
At the first sign of damage, I would quit the game, As it is,
do my body some good.
5.5. Life today was easier because the weather was cooler. In the forest
‚we made our first timid attempts to dig in. The landscape behind Heiligenblut

995a. 4.19. Just returned from a rainy and windy night drill. At six o’clod
we marched in the direction of Moosburg, toward the enemy, and set up ow
bivouac in a favorably located farmhouse. We kept watch not far from ther
Corporal Schuster had a toothache, which made him a bit tough at times, bul
after he had found a protected shelter in the roomy woodshed, we were 0
good terms again and chatted; as usual, he spoke a great deal about the fron
Spying the sergeant (this is always the chief job of a look-out), we quick!
stationed two guards out in the wind. Then we had visits from patrols, tw
fellows as tall as trees belonging to the first detachment who admired ow

it may actually
338 / Diary IV 339 / March 1916 to December 1918
is superb, everything today seemed as if it had been dropped there bya
wizard; again hiked about seven miles,

5.8. Weather cooler, soon the rain will settle in. As a result, had a less
painful time of it. Spent four and a half hours on duty in the magnificen|
forest, during the morning, Unfortunately, couldn’t get rid of my pack. But
it really was quite manageable. .

5.9. Marksmanship competition. Take it easy: this time, I didn’t hit any
thing. At a distance of four hundred yards my slight nearsightedness begins
to tell a bit. I talked myself out of it and was allowed not to lug along any
special sights, We finally ate at three o’clock. On the rifle range I talked: to
Schinnerer and—talked to him at great length, He gave me an apple to quie
my hungry stomach, :

5.11. At five in the morning we had to hike off to the cavalry drillgrounds,
There we took up our places for inspection in the expectation of His Excellency’s arrival. Other sections had their turn before us, while our appearance
got some extra polishing up. We then had to go through some posture exer:
cises, which went off relatively well. Other drills went all haywire. Splendid
May day, not sultry, did its part. No hiking in the afternoon. Tomorr
evening, a night exercise,

5.12. Tomorrow vaccination takes place at 11 in the morning, so that
we can probably take the one o’clock train to Munich, But should I have to
leave on the 5:14 train—which is not 1i ely—I shall not be able to use the
main exit but shall have to get out way over near the Bayerstrasse and, after

some frantic crowding, appear near the Kommandantur in the great crosscorridor.

Sezession has asked leave for me, but
before the thirty-first of May,
everyone must take part.

~ Yesterday the drill was very easy throughout the day. In the evening we
hiked to Klausenberg for a night exercise. We had no packs to carry, and it
was a delightful walk through the dusking forest,

5.23. This morning, awakened by pouring rain, half pleased, half apprehensive. But soon the i

weather cleared again, and it turned very sultry. At,
least the annoying dust has vanished. Out on the infantry drillground, the
usual exercises, then an

attack directed by flag signals. Now a short two hours’

test in bed. Hunger and thirst were great, but the remedy for both was available.

the captain is reluctant to grant any
for we are about to have maneuvers, in which

996. 5.25. From six o’clock till about twelve, experienced and suffered
through a long hike. From one to three o’clock Thad a fine sleep in my bed.
Weather not sultry, but very hot. We had a half-hour’s rest in the forest,
moments of deepest bliss.
5.26... . You complain and have headaches and are pale. I drag myself
about for hours, loaded down, in the bright sun, but I have long since forgotten what those ailments are like. Safely survived the first combat exercise.

Safely, because it is over; the field canteen was a very amusing sight; it was
like being in a kindergarten,

997. 5.30. Routine drill on the infantry drillgrounds,

the most varied sorts of sore spots, owing to the antics o
Then to company formation. The captain wanted to kn,
age would be like. He grants me leave from the first
ourth; from the fifth to the eighth, I must be back ag
o the thirteenth, we’ll have Pentecost leave.

Then we'll be divided into companies,

itive. Promoted a class, I shall be home o
n the first.

Unfortunately, had
f the past few days.
ow what the vernisto the night of the
ain. From the ninth

5.15. Arrived safely. It had stopped raining. This morning, did some ele.
mentary things in drill period, not requiring too much exertion on account
of the weather. In retrospect, I am still delighted with the opera I heard and
am very much for repeating this from time to time. ;

5.18. The final rehearsal of The Creation is on Saturday afternoon at 5.30,
the performance on Sunday at five o’clock, in the Dominican Church (I play
the violin in the orchestra). :

5.19. Walden informs me that the watercolors that I had requested back

" have been returned. He has sold “The Anatomy of Venus,” net gain: one
hundred and sixty marks. He begs for new material to sell . . . The Neue

and a new batch of recruits will
n the evening of the thirty-first or

998. Furlough. The captain sent me a

pass from the fifth to the eighth
without my having applied for it. Now,

I assumed that the pass for Pente-
*

340 / Diary IV

cost (June ninth to thirteenth) would follow automatically. With my civilian
mentality, I thought it unnecessary to return to camp on the night of the
eighth, in order to leave again with the convoy on the ninth. But when, on
the ninth, the pass didn’t arrive, I felt like a green recruit and sent a request
by wire. The answer came: “Return at once.” ae

999. 6.9. Landshut. How terribly funny the whole sad mess is. The closer
I came to Landshut, the gayer I felt; when I stepped off the train, I felt in
fine humor and full of fighting spirit. The familiar way into town. :
In the Heissgarten I was able to have a word in private with a soldier
tached to the administration bureau, who was dusting there. The fellow
thinks there will be no trouble. The whole camp really did steam off at six
this morning. My exception is therefore an irrefutable argument (civilian’s
argument), and I hope to convince the company sergeant tomorrow morning
at nine-thirty, After all, he’s had his fun by making me jump on the train and
come back. So let’s not get excited! If I am really lucky, I shall take the morn
ing express at 10:40; if not, the 1:21. In that case, I’ll send a telegram. _

1000. What happened was that a substitute captain, called Daxelhofer,
thundered at me, or at least tried to, but was put to shame by my eloquent
defense and by my appealing to the authority of Captain Jungwirt. He did
not sentence me, but left this to the regular captain. My punishment fo
the time being shall be the loss of a day. Made out my Pentecost pass and
sent me off. The phenomenon of the captain’s handing out an unrequested
pass was incredible!

1oooa. A half a day has passed. On Saturday we rest again. We haven’
been mixed in with the new recruits, but all belong to groups trained at th
same time. Only the small ones (fourth and fifth section) were sent t
Mitterwehr. There was something really disgraceful about these runts..Ou
platoon now looks very trim. The noncommissioned officers saved my rifl
for me, my other things hang nicely all together, Vize Pritzlmeier, the doc.
_ tor, has remained. A first lieutenant is in charge of us. Company drill toda
was conducted by a very likeable and able second lieutenant named Feh,

_with two cultivated “gentlemen.” Later

341 / March 1916 to December 1918

My section-leader is called No Wimmer. At our company exercise, we were
bout three hundred; it is quite amusing.

portion of pork-brawn jelly for thirty pfennig is worth noting.

6.23. Handed in two passes, an old one and a new one. Had a good trip

had beer and pork-brawn jelly with
tuber and Dr. Vize and a company sergeant I didn’t know. To bed at
twelve. On duty: inspection by the first lieutenant who rode along everyhere with the captain, until the sweat dripped.

002. 6.27. No leave, I expect my family on Wednesday evening. As an
dded misfortune, a night exercise is planned, so that I won’t be able to
pick them up. Fräulein Weidmüller makes tea for them, Lily puts Felix to
ed, and when I return, I find her comfortably settled.

003. 7.1. No furlough, because I got the first watch. Lily and Felix must
ome on Saturday evening. I’ll be free until Sunday noon. Then, sleep at

the barracks (Martin School, now infirmary),

1004. 7.4. Letter from Probst, which pP
ence with Osthaus-Friess-Piper.

From my sentinel’s post, sent a devoted greeting to Lily. Today, shot six,

eased me. Took care of correspond-

twelve, eleven. In the afternoon, “cleaning-and-sewing hour.” At six, long

ke, great fun. After the watch I slept so well in my bed that for the first
time it was the alarm clock that woke me,

What an affair this guard duty is! Changing of the guard! And yet I survived it quite well. A new monotony is added to the old routine. Unfortu-

nately Lily didn’t once open the gate of the infirmary, behind which I stood
all day. At night I paced up and down the sidewalk with my rifle on my

shoulder and listened to the quarter-hours tolling. Two to four in the after5

h 191 D 1
342 / Diary IV 343 / March 1916 to December 1918

MUNICH 1916

006. 7.20. “My dearest Lily. We are here. For the time being, at the Max
igh School, Second Reserve Infantry Regiment, First Replace
any. The others are quartered at the Grosse Wirt, we are in the gym of
ax High School. Because of all the waiting time, it has now got to be
-fourthirty, and the issuing of uniforms is just getting under way very slowly.
\ xchanging my Landshut tags for gray semi-rags. It may therefore take quite
AA : while before my turn comes. So it is unlikely that I'll still see the two of

ment Com-

167

WITHOUT TIILE, 1916, 1

‚1007. Report. Duty wasn’t really stiffer than in Landshut. The long hikes

abit harder. The potshots more on target. Serious combat maneuvers, And
t there is still the same

great confusion as always, Sergeant Feueregger,
at frst amusingly haughty. Gradually a certain bonhomie developed. Major

bleitner, a ridiculous man full of high-flown phrases: “Be glad that you
don’t go off on leave, like peasants, but are allowed to drill and learn here
instead!” Later, frequent watches instead of night exercises and long hikes.

noon, eight to ten at night, two to four in the morning, eight to ten in
forenoon. I have the impression that the war is entering its last phase.

1oo4a. Today, the long hike was made somewhat easier by pleasant weather.
The evening and night hikes were more exhausting. In the afternoon, we ge
going only at about three o’clock, and we usually don’t leave the area, excep!
for a bath. My impression is that a change is pending (transfer), An emer.
gency training period is supposed to start in Freising on the twenty-fifth
perhaps, as a “one-year man,” I shall go there. Perhaps some day I'll loo!
back on the time spent here as a rosy period.

1008. While I was standing

guard over the munitions depot in Fröttmaning,
on one of several occasions,

a great many thoughts about Marc and his art
came to me. Walking in circles around a couple of ammunition storehouses
was just the kind of thing to get one lost in thought. During the day magcent midsummer flora made things unusually colorful, and late at night
ind before dawn, a firmament unfolded before me that lured my soul into
vast expanses,
When I tell what kind of petson Franz Marc is, I must at once confess
that I shall also be telling what kind of person I am, for much that I participated in belonged also to him,
He is more human, he loves more warmly,
‘sponds to animals as if they were human. He

1005. 7.20. Transferred to Munich. Train trip, waiting, Turk School, wail

ing, transfer ceremony, a conceited first lieutenant. Talk, then to the Gross

Wirt inn. Then to the Max Classical High School. Second Reserve, Seventh
~ Regiment, First Replacement-(Field)-Company. Permission to sleep at hom

is more demonstrative. He
granted only after making the grade. .

raises them to his level. He
344 / Diary IV

does not begin by dissolving himself, becoming merely a part in the whole
so as to place himself on the same level with plants and stones and animals
In Marc, the bond with the earth takes precedence over the bond with th
universe (I am not saying that he might not have developed in the directio
of the latter, and yet: Why, in that case, did he die?).

The Faustian element in him, the unredeemed. Forever questioning.
it true? Using the word “heresy.” But lacking the calm assurance of faith
Often, toward the end, I was afraid that he might be a completely different
man some day. a

The changing times oppressed him, he wanted men to change with them
But he himself was still a human being and there was a remnant of inn
conflict that bound him. The bourgeois empire, which was the last instanc
of the good being still a common good, seemed worthy of envy to him.

I only try to relate myself to God, and if I am in harmony with
I don’t fancy that my brothers are not also in harmony with me; but tha
their business.

One of Mare’s traits was a feminine urge to give everyone some of h
treasure, The fact that not everyone followed him filled him with misgivin
about his path. I often anxiously surmised that he would return to earth]
simplicity, once the ferment was over, that he would not come back in’ ord

to rouse the world to some grand vision, but entirely from a human impulse,

My fire is more like that of the dead or of the unborn. No wonder th

he found more love. His noble sensuousness with its warmth attracted man

people to him. He was still a real member of the human race, not a neutr
creature, I recall his smile when my eye overlooked some elements of ea
Art is like Creation: it holds good on the last day as on the first.

‘What my art probably | is a ki
ly lacks, is a kind of i i
nimals and every sort of creatu ith an earthly warmth ae

346 / Diary IV 347 / March 1916 to December 1918
1011. The other lieutenant, a handsome businessman from London,
not very serious, but amusing, The peasants liked him, it seemed to me

I was in no hurry at all about my marksmanship. An instinct bade. me to
be fatalistic about it.

SCHLEISSHEIM 1916
4 1013. And so I was no longer an infantry man. What next? First there
as the trip with myself in charge (there were four of us): Wildermann
and two workers. When we arrived in Schleissheim, a horrible, degraded
sentry wearing a felt shako greeted us. This was already a degree worse than
what we were accustomed to. After all, there had ‚been decorum in the infantry.
This sentry led us silently to the barracks, a building which, though new,
as narrow and small. A sergeant (from the recruits’ depot) took charge of
us, assigned us to our sections, and sent us around. Passport division, etc.
We belonged to the workshop company.
Since we felt safe, we presented ourselves not simply as painters, but as
_ artistic painters. This caused some shaking of heads. Only the company
clerk was pleased. We were going to get work, he said, that would enable
_ us to put our art to the test. Now to our quarters: the church warehouse:
The roll calls, Sergeant Poschenrieder. The yard, the work there. Prolearians. Factory workers. Stubenvoll. Three-week leave.

1012. Gradually I am getting to be popular with the noncoms. The sergean!
of the dormitory recognized in me a wild, “ferocious“ artist. He was a mı
ber of the Artists’ Association. I now realized that I was no longer in thal
God-forsaken, provincial village Landshut. The quartermaster too was
friendly. Gradually the ribbing (though well intentioned) of the comp
sergeant diminished. The company’s chief clerk became intimately friend]
with me. I was put on watch whenever a long hike or a night drill wa
planned. These were my impressions from my lowly estate.
I had long since been spending the nights at home and was beginning t
enjoy my new post when suddenly, on the drillgrounds, during a pause i
one of the innumerable combat exercises, my name was called. “Here!
Report at once to the battalion medical officer, and before that to the se
geant. At once, I sensed something important. The sergeant was in excelle
spirits. “Don’t you want to fly?” “Who, me?” “Yes, didn’t you request.
be put in the air corps?” “Forgive me, Sir, I know nothing about it!” “Wel
just so you will understand, I’ll tell you that we put in the request for yo
Now go and see the doctor to find out whether you're fit for it.” At the do
tor’s office a few more men were waiting. One of them asked me wheth
I was Klee. Introduced himself as Wildermann. First came the doctor's a
sistant. Did I nave anything to say? No! Then came the medical officer. W:
I ready to give up my one-year engagement? In reply, I asked: are there.
one-year men in the air corps? He shrugged his shoulders slightly.
When I returned to the sergeant I had already been transferred. He co:
gratulated me: “Be glad that you're getting out of the miserable infan!
Now go and eat right away, then get ready. Here are your papers. You
receive further instructions at the Türkenschule.” He shook my hand. “Goo
bye, Klee, take care of yourself!” The other noncoms were e grinning too. |
clerk smiled as he shook my hand.

1014. August 12, 1916. From: Engineer Klee, Air Force Reserve Unit,
Schleissheim, Workshop Company. To: Frau Lily Klee, Munich, Ainmillerstrasse 32, second floor.in the garden pavilion. “What a disappointment that
you didn’t come. Well, let us hope for Sunday; saw Stubenvoll, promised
~he'd help. But sleeping outside camp is impossible. Will you find a place?

verything is said to be full to overflowing with pilots, Well see. They
have me varnishing wings, It isn’t exactly dangerous. But suddenly to be
turned into a factory worker, how adventurous! And the garb! Yours, Paul.”

1015. 10.4. “My dear Lily! All my thanks for your last card from Switzerand. But most of all, welcome, both of you, to Munich! Unfortunately I
couldn’t get leave. I am on guard duty from Wednesday to Thursday, that

comes first here. I hope both of you had a good return trip, and that the
boy didn’t catch a cold.”
March 1916 to December 1918
348 / Diary IV 349 / Mare

10214. Beginning of November. “Since I had had leave, it was felt in the
yard that I ought to be on fire guard this Sunday. I asked the sergeant about
it, He said he was sorry about it, but they had put my name on the list out
at the yard. It certainly isn’t his fault. All day Sunday one is restricted to
two rooms in the barracks and to the canteen; it is no use, therefore, to pay
me a visit.

Otherwise I am well, still constantly braced against army trickery, But the
time for compensation will come again, which will make the Saxon foreman
furious. Tonight (Saturday) I will work a little; I have some oil and a warm

1016. 10.5. “In spite of your special delivery letter and telegram, I cannot:
ask for a leave twice in the same week. As if free will still existed! It’s not
wise to attract attention with small requests. See you Saturday! The train no

eaves from here.at 6:30 p.m.; so, either seven at the station, or 7:30 at home

1017. 10.12. “The captain will grant me leave after the slip has been signed
by the supervisor at the yard. It will probably be Friday.” :

1018. 10.23. “Arrived safely. Varnished two wooden fuselages. Corrected:
old numbers on airplanes, painted on new ones in front with the help of
emplates. While at work, had the impression that the Saxon foreman was . tine, I have been placed in
sneaking around in my vicinity. Rumor of a drill spread at four o’clock. Then 1022. 11.13. To liven up my monotonous rou me, 1 isit
seized a favorable moment and escaped from the column. Cained a nice charge of a transport to Cologne. I only regret that I'll have to wht tories ws t
chunk of time thereby. What the consequences will be, we'll see. Until now: tomorrow, Tuesday, since I am leaving tonight from the freight a ith .
I've been a virtuous recruit, gentle and obedient, All I got for it was a bungled Milbertshofen. But the business is amusing; I have been entrusted with air

i h
leave. Result: I have ceased being a virtuous recruit. I have started coloring planes for Fea Seven. I was given a fine chunk of bacon and money for the
he lithograph for Goltz.” journey.

je

019. 10.26. “Friday from four to five, fire drill, then off to rollcall. Had
hard time sneaking away. Not in my room before six o’clock. And so now:

here’s a bad boy in the family, Should I have to stand guard on Sunday, I
hall have to be back here at g a.m.”

FIRST TRANSPORT

2er

; 1oz2za. 11.13. (Monday). At four p.m., walked, loaded with food, to Milbertshofen; found three airplanes there almost loaded. At 10 p.m., left by
freight train for Laim.

1020, 11.2.

“‘T send you a warm greeting. Hope to see you again next Saturday. Today was a splendid day. I was kept outdoors for an hour and a half,

taking care of trucks while the motors were being unloaded. Not bad for
one’s health anyway.”

1023. 11.14. Left Laim at 2 a.m. Breakfasted in Treuchtlingen. In the afternoon, coffee and buns in Würzburg. Reached Aschaffenburg at 9:30 p.m.,
ate very well at the station. Stayed there until 3:45 a.m. Spent this time
on the floor of the guard room, wrapped in a traveling blanket. I show
naive enthusiasm, so I’ll soon be sent on a transport trip again. I dream about
a journey to the Balkans. I sit beside the engineer and often think of Felix
and of the fun he would have here, since he is still hesitating between becoming a locomotive engineer and a painter.

1021. 11.10, “Guard duty on Sunday, beginning at noon; I shall have to be

back at 9 a.m. Today, had to help clean up the wrecked airplane in which
two flyers lost their lives day before yesterday. Badly damaged motor, etc.;
really inspiring work. I told Poschenrieder that I am often called on to help

Section Two. He promises to stand by me. At least I shall have had the.
experience of the crashed plane.”
350 / Diary IV 351 / March 1916 to December 1918

1024. 11.15. Left Aschaffenburg in the morning,
ple of hours in the waiting room of the station, To Grossgerau, Mainz; pause
at Bingen, a very elegant place on the Rhine. A unique mixture of nature
and culture. The mountains, the river, the harbor installations, the ships,
the terraces. Mainz had made a great impression on me, But there, the
artistic element outweighs all the others, If I could only stay for a while in
Bingen as an artist! Tonight we'll be in Cologne. Unfortunately I have no
money with me, except the twenty-five marks for my expenses,

and quarters for the night at the Hippodrom. Taken there and brought back
ina special trolley car. j

_ At eight a.m., left for Munich via Würzburg and Nürnberg. In Munich
at.6 a.m. On to Schleissheim at 6:30; since there was no on in the office, I
hastened away to save my Sunday leave. Walked home by way of Milbertshofen. Here, met a very pleasant company having tea: Hausmann and Dr.
Keller (art dealings).

1025. 11.16. Cologne-Eifeltor, Cologne-Gereon, Cologne-Nippes, Longerich. SECOND TRANSPORT
Delivered airplanes for 249 (Fea Seven). At 4:30 p.m., walked to Bickendorf,
took the trolley from there to Cathedral Square, had my hair cut, and

then went to the Hotel Kélner Hof. My mission is accomplished. It was a

o26a. To Fighter Squadron 5 with two airplanes, cars 37090 Hanover and
4059 Konigsberg, via Cologne-Gereon, staging area. Planes B.F.W. 3054/16
nd 3053/16 with spare parts,

11.27. The transport won't begin until tonight, Monday. Perhaps I can
et home for a moment from Milbertshofen to catch up on a few things.
11.28. In the morning, I’m off, completely equipped as if I were leaving
or the front, including rifle. On arriving in Milbertshofen, I find that the
irplanes are not yet teady to leave. The train will not leave until 9 pm.
deposit my bags with the stationmaster and trot off home, where I am still
ble to have a comfortable lunch and tea.

11.29. This time I have trouble getting out of Munich. I am stuck in
Moosach.

I spend the night on a wooden bench in the waiting room, The line to
aim closed as the result of a minor accident. We have to wait until 3:30
n, We must remain on the train, for no one can predict exactly when it will
nove on. In Laim we wait some more, until 9:30 p.m., when train 1755 is
eady at last, Fortunately I took along plenty of food; perhaps it will make
etting away easier. The baggage car is relatively comfortable; I set up camp

ere and had an excellent sleep until Treuchtlingen. Here the baggage car
as. switched,

day evening. I once again spend a civilized night in a nice hotel room
Tomorrow I’ll be on my own. Took a wonderful walk in the evening.

1026. 11.17. Slept magnificently at the hotel, after having led a dirty.
8ypsy’s life since last Sunday. Cologne is sensational, polished to a smoothness, and large, Particularly impressed by it last night! The affluence in the
main streets, those people in uniform! The mad railroad station. Right in
front of it, that more-than-lifesize museum piece, the cathedral. The Hohenzollern bridge, totally dark and heavily guarded. The river. The sharp beams
of four wily searchlights. Far above the towers of the cathedral, the bright.
little bar of a Zeppelin, maneuvering gracefully, speared by one of the beams
I had never seen any city put on such a nocturnal spectacle, truly a solemn’
festival of evil, ;
Stroll over the bridges. Had coffee for breakfast. Museum: Bosch, Breu-..
ghel, “Crucifixion” by the Master of the “Life of Mary.” Cathedral. Lunched’ /
at the Red Cross by the main railroad station, then went to the Artists’
Association, then to the Deutsche Ring, as a courtesy to Wildermann, and:
had a look at his sculptures on the children’s playground. Strolled along the
Rhine, pastry shop, dinner again at the station, left for Frankfurt at 8 p.m,

11.18, Arrived in Frankfurt-am-Main at night, idyllic welcome with dinne y impressions of Spessart again very intense. Heiligenbriicken! Reached

Ingres is said to have created an artistic order out of rest;
I should like to create an order from feeling and,
going
still further, from motion.
Paul Klee, September 1914

is a translation by Mrs E. Lewis-lsastid
of the

In this edition by kind permission of

For ease of reference, where there are captions to drawings and diagrams,
glossaries are provided wherever necessary. These usually appear on the same page
as the captions to which they refer, but to simplify matters the colour terminology
which recurs throughout pages 465-511
is treated
in a pull-out glossary which appears
between pages 464 and 465.
Editor's Note
Klee himself was eager that the articles and lecture notes from his Bauhaus
period should be published. His widow,
Lily Klee-Stumpf (1876-1946), made every effort
to help us
in carrying out this wish. Her advice was of the utmost help to us
in
establishing certain of the texts and

sections.title: in deciding between variant readings. We are
sections.text:
greatly indebted to the artist's son Felix Klee and to the Paul Klee Stiftung
in Berne
for the loan of many works and for valuable documentation.
We are indebted to Petra Petitpierre for her shorthand notes 'From Paul Klee's
Painting Classes at the Dessau Bauhaus', 'From the Kunstakademie Dusseldorf
1930-32' and 'Klee's Course in Theory'. All these have been used as source material.
Numerous Klee collectors have helped us
in our work. Dr Richard Doetsch-Benziger
donated a colour chart in memory of his friendship with Klee and placed his collection
of reproductions at our disposal. We are indebted to Mr and Mrs Rolf Bijrgi for
numerous loans, to Dr H. Meyer-Benteli for permission to include the Jena lecture
('On Modern Art'), to the OfFentliche Kunstsammlung, Basle, and to Dr Charlotte
Weidler for their helpfulness.
Werner Allenbach, Ida Bienert, Dr
E. Friedrich, Susanne Feigel,
Fritz Gygi,
Daniel-Henry Kahnweiler, the Galerie Rosengart in Lucerne, Hermann Rupf and
G. David Thompson have permitted us to make use of originals and reproductions
from their collections; we wish to thank them and many unnamed lenders.
Robert BiJchler, Christian Overstolz, Kurt Reiss, and a number of friends interested
in
Klee's work have played a large part in the conception and execution of this volume.
J.S.
